title: Bunk Bed Steps with Built-In Drawers
summary: A staircase for the top bunk that's secretly a dresser — four drawers ride in the risers, so the safest way up also swallows the laundry pile.
description:
A ladder is how bunk beds come; stairs are what parents retrofit after the third 2 a.m. climb-down. This build makes the upgrade pay double: each step's riser bay holds a drawer on euro slides — three 24" drawers and one 20" up top — so the staircase stores as much as a small dresser while being vastly safer to descend half-asleep.

The construction is a stack: 1x10s layered longest-at-the-bottom form the stepped side panels, faced with 1x3s, treaded in 1x12. The material trick is in the treads/framing line of the cut list — each 1x10 @ 24" gets a 1-1/2" strip ripped off (that strip becomes cross framing) and the remaining ~7-3/4" piece is RESERVED as a drawer face. Zero waste, and the drawer faces automatically match the treads.

Drawer-slide alignment is the build's precision requirement, and the plan's method is good: mark 7" down from each row's top on both side panels, center the slides' holes on the lines, and the left-right pairing takes care of itself. Build the side panels in MIRROR. And the finish note earns its place: poly the treads — sock traffic will find raw stain in a week.

tools.note: The 1-1/2" rips that turn tread stock into cross framing plus drawer faces. A circular saw with a guide substitutes.
Panels, treads, back, and drawer boxes — most of the build.
The 7" slide lines on every row — the drawers' entire alignment system.

materials.name: 1x10, 10 ft
materials.note: Side panel rows and tread stock — cut the longest pieces first.

materials.name: 1x12, 12 ft
materials.note: Treads and landing. (Shave the stair width 1/4" and a 10-footer works.)

materials.name: Euro drawer slides, 24", white
materials.note: Bottom-corner-mount style — forgiving to install and cheap.

materials.name: Stain and poly topcoat
materials.note: Poly on the treads is a wear requirement, not a nicety.

cutList.note: Two each at 27-3/4", 18-1/2", and 9-1/4" — the stepped verticals.
Rip 1-1/2" off each for cross framing; the remainder becomes the drawer faces.

steps.title: Build the two stepped side panels — in mirror
steps.body:
Stack the 1x10 rows longest at the bottom, glue between every board, and face with the 1x3s — outside edges flush, glue and 1-1/4" brads. The 1x3 facing is what turns four loose boards into one stair-profiled panel.

Build the second panel in MIRROR. Left stair side and right stair side; two identical panels build a staircase that only works from one side of the bed.

steps.title: Mount the drawer slides on the 7" lines
steps.body: On each panel, measure 7" down from the top of each row and strike a line. Mount the cabinet halves of the slides with their screw holes CENTERED on the lines — the lines do the left-right matching for you. The 20" slide goes on the top row; add blocking behind any slide that needs it.

steps.title: Rip the tread stock — save the offcuts
steps.body: Cut the 1x10 tread stock at 24", rip 1-1/2" off each piece, and set the wide remainders aside labeled DRAWER FACES. Attach the ripped strips as cross framing between the two side panels with glue and brads — the stairs become one structure here.

steps.title: Lay the treads
steps.body: Glue and brad the 1x12 treads and top landing on, outside edges flush, fastening into the back cross strips as well. Add screws where the brads feel lonely — treads take full body weight at a stomp, and the source added them too.

steps.title: Back it
steps.body: Glue and 3/4"-brad the 1/4" plywood back on, edges flush. On a staircase kids will treat as a jungle gym, the back panel's anti-racking job is real structure.

steps.title: Drawers: boxes, slides, faces
steps.body:
Build the four drawer boxes with 1-1/4" brads and glue, bottoms on with 3/4" brads and glue. Mount the drawer halves of the slides, and test every drawer for smooth travel BEFORE facing.

Then the faces — the reserved 1x10 offcuts: tack each with brads at an even reveal, then lock from inside with 1-1/4" screws. Sand face edges anywhere the slide is tight.

Fill, sand, stain, and poly the treads hard. Then push it against the bunk, fill the drawers with pajamas, and retire the ladder with honors.

images.alt: Wooden bunk bed staircase with storage drawers built into the risers
